food_subgroup: Noodle soup dishes with meat and offal

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Lanzhou beef tripe noodles. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0GXM (beef soup with noodles) is the closest available parent. No specific Lanzhou-style beef tripe noodle code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to Lanzhou beef tripe noodles as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not cover this level of culinary specificity.

preparation_method: assembled in bowl with sliced beef and tripe

serving.natural_language_portion: One large bowl of Lanzhou beef and beef tripe noodles, approximately 650 g, comprising hand-pulled wheat noodles, sliced beef, beef tripe, and beef bone broth with chilli oil and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Beef noodle soup, Chinese style — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Beef, shank, cooked (FDC ID 174032); Beef tripe, cooked (FDC ID 169451); Wheat noodles, cooked (FDC ID 169737); Beef broth (FDC ID 171532); adjusted for large-bowl serving composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Lanzhou beef and beef tripe noodles as a composite dish. Per-100g values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 35% cooked wheat noodles, 20% cooked beef shank, 15% cooked beef tripe, 28% beef broth, 2% chilli oil and garnishes. Energy and macros cross-checked against published Chinese beef noodle soup nutritional analyses in peer-reviewed literature (Nutrients journal, 2021 Chinese dietary survey data).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and mixed beef noodle soup — analogous estimate based on: 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021' (Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021); wheat noodles GI ~52 (boiled); composite dish GI estimated at 52 accounting for protein and fat co-ingestion lowering effective GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lanzhou beef and beef tripe noodles. GI estimated at 52 based on boiled wheat noodles GI (52, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The presence of protein (beef, tripe) and fat (broth, chilli oil) in the composite dish is expected to maintain or slightly reduce the effective GI relative to plain noodles. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 68.25 g available carbohydrate per 650 g serving) / 100 = 35.5, rounded to 35.8 accounting for fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from beef bone broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single large bowl provides an estimated 2730 mg sodium (420 mg per 100 g × 650 g), which exceeds the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, consume only half the soup, or avoid this dish on high-sodium days.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles partially offset by protein and fat co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 36 per large bowl is moderate-to-high for a single meal; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion, request extra vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose, as the wheat noodle base raises blood glucose despite the protein and fat buffering effect.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and tripe, and high sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (approximately 7.2 g per large bowl) and very high sodium (approximately 2730 mg per large bowl) presents a dual cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, avoid drinking the broth, and balance this meal with low-sodium, low-saturated-fat choices throughout the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lanzhou Beef and Beef Tripe Noodles (Large) is a Chinese noodle soup dish originating from Lanzhou, the capital of Gansu Province in northwestern China. It is one of China's most iconic street foods and is widely served in Chinese noodle restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of hand-pulled wheat noodles (la mian) served in a rich, slow-simmered beef bone broth seasoned with spices including cumin, star anise, and coriander seed. The large-bowl version (大碗) features generous portions of thinly sliced braised beef (typically shank or brisket) and tender beef tripe (honeycomb or blanket tripe), garnished with white radish slices, spring onion, fresh coriander, and a drizzle of house-made chilli oil. The noodles are characteristically chewy and elastic due to the hand-pulling technique. The broth is clear, amber-coloured, and deeply savoury. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density of approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution from both beef and tripe (7.8 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from the wheat noodles (10.5 g per 100 g), and relatively low fat (3.2 g per 100 g). The primary clinical concern is the high sodium content of the broth, which can exceed the WHO daily recommended limit in a single serving. The dish is halal-certified at many Singapore outlets. It is suitable for individuals seeking a high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate meal but requires sodium management for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

Can I eat beef tripe noodles if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Lanzhou beef and beef tripe noodles as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central individual ingredient entries (beef shank, beef tripe, wheat noodles, beef broth) and cross-checked against published Chinese dietary survey data. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (boiled wheat noodles) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked beef shank (FDC 174032), cooked beef tripe (FDC 169451), cooked wheat noodles (FDC 169737), and beef broth (FDC 171532), weighted at approximately 35:20:15:28:2 (noodles:beef:tripe:broth:oil/garnish). 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients at meaningful levels and is not typically measured in Chinese noodle soup composite analyses. 3. Sodium estimate of 420 mg per 100 g reflects a typical restaurant-prepared beef noodle broth; home-prepared versions may have substantially lower sodium. 4. The large-bowl serving size of 650 g is estimated based on typical Singaporean Chinese noodle restaurant large-bowl portions; actual restaurant portions may vary. 5. GI is estimated at 52 (low category) based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite dish GI may be slightly lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion. 6. Halal status reflects the restaurant context (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles); individual outlet halal certification should be verified. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component USDA data and should be treated as approximate.
